questions.q: Which pair of problems with Rutherford's nuclear model was the Bohr model designed to overcome?
options: The proton's mass and the neutron's discovery
Atomic stability (the electron should spiral in) and the line spectrum (atoms should emit a continuous, not discrete, spectrum)
The charge-to-mass ratio and the elementary charge
The size of the nucleus and the speed of the electron
questions.explanation: The 'two S's': by Maxwell an orbiting electron is an accelerating charge that should radiate, spiral in, and collapse the atom (Stability), and would emit a continuously rising frequency (Spectrum) — yet atoms are stable and emit discrete lines. Bohr's model fixes both.

questions.q: What does Bohr's first postulate assert about an electron in a stationary state?
options: It radiates electromagnetic energy continuously as Maxwell requires
It orbits the nucleus without radiating any electromagnetic energy
It is at rest at the centre of the atom
It can have any energy at all
questions.explanation: Bohr's audacious first postulate is that in certain allowed orbits (stationary states) the electron orbits without radiating, flatly contradicting Maxwell. This single decree fixes the stability crisis: the ground-state electron has no lower state to fall to, so it cannot spiral in.

questions.q: Bohr's second postulate states that when an electron moves between two stationary states it emits or absorbs a single photon. The photon's energy equals:
options: The sum of the energies of the two states
The kinetic energy of the electron
The difference in energy between the two states, with \(hf = E_{\text{high}} - E_{\text{low}}\)
Planck's constant divided by the orbit radius
questions.explanation: By conservation of energy the photon carries exactly the energy difference between the levels: \(hf = E_{\text{high}} - E_{\text{low}}\). This borrows Einstein's photon (energy \(hf\)) from the photoelectric effect to carry energy in and out of the atom on each jump.

questions.q: Why is the Bohr model described as a mixture of classical and quantum physics?
options: It uses only Newtonian mechanics throughout
It keeps a classical circular orbit held by the Coulomb force as the centripetal force, but bolts on quantum rules (allowed orbits, no radiation, photon jumps)
It treats the electron purely as a wave with no orbit
It abandons the photon in favour of continuous radiation
questions.explanation: The classical half is the planetary orbit with the electrostatic Coulomb force providing the centripetal force; the quantum half is the three rules — only certain orbits allowed, no radiation in an orbit, and photon-sized energy jumps. This inelegant hybrid is itself listed as a limitation.

questions.q: Why does each element produce a unique line spectrum?
options: Each element emits light of a single colour set by its mass
Each element has a unique set of energy levels, so unique energy gaps, so a unique set of photon wavelengths
Heavier elements emit shorter wavelengths only
Spectral lines depend on the temperature, not the element
questions.explanation: An element's energy levels are fixed by its nuclear charge and electron arrangement. Unique levels mean unique energy differences between them, and since \(E = hf\) fixes the frequency (and wavelength) of each photon, each element emits and absorbs only its own fingerprint of wavelengths.

questions.q: On an energy-level diagram, an arrow pointing UP between two levels represents:
options: Emission of a photon as the electron loses energy
Absorption of a photon as the electron gains energy
Ionisation of the atom
The electron staying in the same level
questions.explanation: 'Up you Absorb, Down you Emit.' An upward arrow means the electron gains energy by absorbing a photon to climb the ladder; a downward arrow means it loses energy by emitting a photon. Energies are negative, with the ground state most negative at the bottom.

questions.q: A larger energy gap between two levels produces a photon with:
options: Lower frequency and longer wavelength
Higher frequency and shorter wavelength
The same frequency but greater intensity
Zero frequency
questions.explanation: A bigger gap means a bigger photon energy, so by \(E = hf\) a higher frequency, and by \(c = f\lambda\) a shorter wavelength. The largest jumps (down to the ground state, the Lyman series) give the highest-energy ultraviolet photons.

questions.q: In hydrogen, electron transitions that end on the \(n = 2\) level form which series, and in which region of the spectrum?
options: The Lyman series, in the ultraviolet
The Paschen series, in the infrared
The Balmer series, in the visible
The Balmer series, in the ultraviolet
questions.explanation: 'Little Birds Pause': Lyman (to n=1, UV), Balmer (to n=2, visible), Paschen (to n=3, infrared). The Balmer series is the only one in visible light; its famous red H-alpha line at 656 nm comes from the n=3 → n=2 transition.

questions.q: Using the Rydberg equation, the n=3 → n=2 transition in hydrogen gives \(\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097\times10^{7}\left(\dfrac{1}{4}-\dfrac{1}{9}\right)\). What is the wavelength, and what common error must be avoided?
options: About \(1.5\times10^{6}\ \mathrm{m}\); no special care needed
About \(656\ \mathrm{nm}\); the equation gives \(1/\lambda\), so you must take the reciprocal to get \(\lambda\)
About \(656\ \mathrm{m}\); convert from nanometres at the end
About \(122\ \mathrm{nm}\); take the reciprocal twice
questions.explanation: \(\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097\times10^{7}\times0.1389 = 1.524\times10^{6}\ \mathrm{m^{-1}}\), so \(\lambda = 1/(1.524\times10^{6}) = 6.56\times10^{-7}\ \mathrm{m} = 656\ \mathrm{nm}\). The Rydberg constant is in \(\mathrm{m^{-1}}\), so the equation returns \(1/\lambda\); forgetting to invert is the most common error (an answer near \(10^{6}\) is not a wavelength).

questions.q: Which of the following is a genuine limitation of the Bohr model?
options: It cannot predict the spectral lines of hydrogen
It works only for hydrogen and other one-electron systems and cannot explain multi-electron atoms
It assumes the electron has no charge
It predicts a continuous spectrum instead of discrete lines
questions.explanation: 'Hydrogen Is My Sole Why': it works only for hydrogen/one-electron systems, can't explain line Intensities, can't explain line splitting (Magnetic/Zeeman effect), is a Sticky classical-quantum mixture, and never explains Why orbits are stable and non-radiating. It does correctly predict hydrogen's lines and discrete spectrum.
